flashcards.Back: A standardized, on-scene emergency management tool designed to allow users to adopt an integrated organizational structure. It minimizes duplication of effort and ensures efficient resource management.
The individual who has overall responsibility for managing the entire incident. They establish objectives and have final authority over all resource decisions.
A structure where representatives from multiple agencies work together to develop a common set of objectives. It prevents conflicts in command and ensures a coordinated response.
The ratio of supervisors to subordinates, ideally maintained at $1$ to $5$. This ensures that every leader can effectively manage their assigned resources.
A command staff member responsible for identifying hazardous situations and ensuring the safety of all personnel. They have the authority to stop any unsafe operation immediately.
The individual responsible for providing accurate and timely information to the media and public. They serve as the single point of contact for incident-related updates.
The point of contact for assisting or coordinating agencies participating in the incident. They handle requests for resources from outside organizations.
The component of the General Staff responsible for managing all tactical operations at the incident. They carry out the Incident Action Plan to achieve mission objectives.
The section responsible for collecting, evaluating, and disseminating information about the incident's status. They develop the Incident Action Plan and maintain resource status documentation.
The section responsible for providing facilities, services, and materials needed to support the incident. This includes food, medical supplies, and communications equipment.
The section responsible for tracking all incident costs, including payroll, equipment rental, and injury compensation. They are typically only activated for large or complex incidents.
An emergency situation that involves three or more patients or places a great demand on available resources. It requires the use of triage and the Incident Command System.
A protocol used to quickly categorize adult patients at an $MCI$ based on $RPM$ criteria. It stands for Simple Triage and Rapid Treatment.
A modified triage system designed specifically for pediatric patients at a mass-casualty incident. It accounts for the physiological differences in children, such as respiratory rates.
Patients with life-threatening injuries who require immediate care and transport. Examples include airway obstruction, severe bleeding, or tension pneumothorax.
Patients with significant injuries who can wait for a short period before receiving treatment. Examples include stable fractures or large wounds without severe bleeding.
Often called the walking wounded, these patients have minor injuries and can move to a designated area. They are the last priority for transport.
Patients who are already dead or have injuries so catastrophic that survival is not possible. They are not moved or treated until all other patients are cleared.
The three assessments performed during $START$ triage: Respirations, Perfusion, and Mental Status. A failure in any one category results in a Red tag.
If a patient is not breathing, the airway is opened once; if they still do not breathe, they are tagged Black. If they breathe over $30$ times per minute, they are tagged Red.
Evaluated using capillary refill or radial pulse. If capillary refill is greater than $2$ seconds or the radial pulse is absent, the patient is tagged Red.
The patient is asked to follow a simple command. If they cannot follow the command or are unconscious, they are tagged Red.
The legal requirement for emergency vehicle operators to drive with safety for all others on the road. It does not grant absolute immunity from traffic laws.
The safe distance maintained between an ambulance and other vehicles on the road. This space allows for reaction time and prevents collisions during sudden stops.
A psychological phenomenon where the sound of a siren causes a driver to increase speed and take unnecessary risks. It can lead to a false sense of security.
Areas around the ambulance that cannot be seen in the mirrors. Drivers must lean forward and look around the mirrors to ensure these areas are clear.
The process of removing dirt and organic matter from surfaces using soap and water. It is the first step in the decontamination process.
The use of a chemical agent to kill most bacteria and viruses on non-critical surfaces. It is typically performed after every call involving patient contact.
A more intense disinfection process that kills all microorganisms except for high numbers of bacterial spores. It is used for items like laryngoscope blades.
The use of heat or chemicals to kill all forms of microbial life, including spores. This process is required for any equipment that enters the body or sterile tissues.
The area immediately surrounding a hazardous materials release where the highest risk of contamination exists. Access is restricted to personnel with specialized protective equipment.
The area where personnel and equipment decontamination takes place. It serves as a buffer between the contaminated hot zone and the safe cold zone.
The safe area where the command post and support functions are located. No contaminated personnel or equipment are allowed in this zone.
A reference manual used to identify hazards and determine initial safety distances at a hazmat incident. It is organized by chemical name and $ID$ number.
A document provided by manufacturers that lists the physical properties, hazards, and first-aid measures for a specific chemical. It must be available in workplaces.
A color-coded sign used to identify the health, flammability, and reactivity hazards of materials. Numbers range from $0$ (no hazard) to $4$ (severe hazard).
A $24$-hour emergency resource that provides technical information to responders dealing with hazardous materials incidents. They can be contacted via phone or radio.
The physical or chemical process of removing or neutralizing contaminants from people and equipment. It prevents the spread of toxins into clean areas.
Permission given by a conscious, competent adult for medical care. It must be informed, meaning the patient understands the risks and benefits of treatment.
A legal principle that assumes an unconscious or mentally incapacitated patient would want life-saving treatment. It applies when a threat to life or limb exists.
Consent obtained from a third party, such as a law enforcement officer or legal guardian, for a patient who cannot legally consent. This often applies to psychiatric emergencies.
A failure to provide the expected standard of care, resulting in injury to the patient. It requires duty to act, breach of duty, damages, and causation.
The termination of patient care without the patient's consent or without transferring care to a provider of equal or higher training. It is a legal and ethical violation.
The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act, which protects the privacy of patient health information. It limits the disclosure of sensitive medical data.
A legal obligation to provide care when an individual is on duty or when a specific relationship exists. For EMTs, this usually begins when they are dispatched.
The level of care that a reasonable and prudent person with the same training would provide in a similar situation. It is defined by local and national guidelines.
The legal boundaries of what an EMT is permitted to do based on their certification level. It is determined by state law and medical oversight.
Gaining entry to a vehicle or structure without the use of specialized tools. This typically involves opening an unlocked door or rolling down a window.
Gaining entry to a vehicle or structure using tools such as hydraulic cutters or glass breakers. It requires specialized training and equipment.
A flat, clear area for a helicopter landing, ideally measuring $100$ feet by $100$ feet. The surface must be free of loose debris and overhead wires.
